title: One thing that genuinely surprised us building a stock ranking system:
author: StockGPTpro
selftext:
How insanely sensitive the rankings became to tiny weight changes.



At one point we were adjusting certain signal weights by literally 0.001% at a time…



and the top rankings would completely reshuffle.



You’d slightly rebalance:

\- post-earnings drift weighting

\- cross-sector relative strength decay

\- volatility-adjusted momentum contribution

\- institutional accumulation signals

\- forward revision sensitivity



…and suddenly:

\- a stock jumps from #52 to #4

\- sector concentration changes entirely

\- turnover increases dramatically

\- beta exposure drifts

\- or the whole ranking system becomes unintentionally momentum-heavy



At first we thought something was broken.



But eventually we realized that’s just the reality of trying to rank 500 highly efficient companies against each other simultaneously.



The differences between many names are marginal, which honestly changed the way we think about “AI stock prediction” in general.



A lot of people imagine some model discovering hidden magical signals.



In practice, a huge amount of the work became:

\- balancing signal orthogonality

\- managing factor overlap

\- controlling regime sensitivity

\- and preventing the system from becoming overreactive to short-term dislocations



The strange part is sometimes improving the backtest statistically made the live rankings materially worse, especially when the system became too sensitive to transient market structure shifts.



The deeper we got into this, the less it felt like building a prediction engine and the more it felt like designing a probabilistic ranking framework under constantly changing conditions.

Curious if anyone else building cross-sectional systems has experienced the same level of sensitivity to tiny parameter adjustments.
